key_insight: A brief Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) program was feasible and well accepted for people at risk of inherited prion disease, with early signs of improved psychological well-being.
clinical_relevance: This suggests scalable, low-burden psychological support may help address distress in individuals identified as genetically at risk for neurodegenerative disease.
method: Outcomes measured at baseline, 1 month, and 3 months plus qualitative interviews
evidence_signal: In a 23-participant feasibility study, all predefined feasibility criteria were met, retention at 3 months was 79%, missing data were low (10%), and acceptability was reported as high.
watch_item: Findings are preliminary from a small, single-clinic study without a control group, so effectiveness and generalizability remain uncertain.
paper_title: A Brief Acceptance and Commitment Therapy Intervention to Support Those Living At Risk of Inherited Prion Disease
